metadata.summary: Test-and-scale framework for paid ads: calculate LTV:CAC ratio, start with one channel, kill underperformers by CTR and CPA rules, scale winners gradually with human checkpoints.

content.title: Paid Acquisition: Unit Economics and Test-and-Scale Framework
content.summary: A test-and-scale framework for profitable paid advertising: calculate LTV:CAC before spending, start with one channel, run 3-5 audience and creative variations with $20-50 per ad before judging, kill ads with CTR below 0.5% after 1,000 impressions, and scale only when CPA stays below target for 3+ consecutive days.

why.p: Most paid acquisition failures share the same root causes: spending before knowing unit economics, testing too few variations, and scaling before statistical confidence. The LTV:CAC ratio (minimum 3:1) is the decision gate that prevents burning budget on campaigns that can never be profitable.

list.item: Launching the first paid acquisition campaign for a product
Choosing which ad platform to start on (Google, Meta, LinkedIn, TikTok)
Building a weekly optimization routine for an active campaign
Diagnosing why an existing campaign is unprofitable
Product does not yet have measurable LTV — fix pricing/retention first
No conversion tracking installed — set up pixels and events before spending
Organic channels not yet tested — paid amplifies a working message; it does not create one

section.name: unit-economics
section.title: Paid Acquisition Unit Economics
section.p: LTV, CAC, and target CPA calculation rules — the prerequisite for any paid acquisition spend.

rule.statement: Calculate LTV:CAC ratio before spending any budget. If LTV:CAC is below 3:1, fix product pricing or retention before launching paid acquisition.
rule.rationale: Paid acquisition at an unfavorable LTV:CAC ratio compounds losses. Every dollar spent accelerates cash burn without building a sustainable business.

example.note: SaaS: ARPU $50/mo x 12 months average lifetime = $600 LTV. Target CAC = $600 / 3 = $200. If conversion rate is 3%, max CPA = $200 x 0.03 = $6. Any campaign delivering CPA above $6 is unprofitable.
B2C app: LTV $10, CAC $8 = 1.25:1 ratio. Scaling this campaign burns cash — each conversion costs 80% of the customer's lifetime value before accounting for product costs.

section.title: Metric Formulas

list.item: LTV = ARPU x Average customer lifetime (months or years)
Target CAC = LTV / 3
Max CPA = Target CAC x Conversion rate
ROAS = Revenue / Ad Spend (minimum target: 3:1)

rule.statement: Do not launch paid acquisition until pixel/conversion tracking is installed and conversion events are firing correctly.
rule.rationale: Without tracking, you cannot measure CPA or ROAS, making optimization impossible and budget decisions arbitrary.

section.name: channel-selection-and-testing
section.title: Channel Selection, Testing Framework, and Creative
section.p: Platform comparison by use case and budget, testing structure, ad copy formulas, and kill/scale decision rules.

rule.statement: Start with ONE channel. Master it before adding more. Spreading budget across multiple channels at low spend prevents any single channel from generating enough data to optimize.
rule.rationale: Each platform has a learning phase requiring minimum conversion volume. Split budgets delay learning and produce inconclusive data on all channels simultaneously.

list.item: Google Ads: high intent (search), $500/mo minimum — best for solution-aware buyers
Meta Ads: B2C, broad demographic targeting, $500/mo minimum — best for awareness to conversion
LinkedIn Ads: B2B by job title and company size, $1000/mo minimum — best for enterprise leads
TikTok Ads: young demographic, viral content, $500/mo minimum — high creative demand

rule.statement: Give each ad a minimum of $20-50 spend and 1,000 impressions before judging performance. Killing ads earlier produces random results.
rule.rationale: Ad delivery needs a learning period. Decisions made on fewer than 1,000 impressions have 15-30% random variance in CTR.

list.item: Start with 3-5 audiences per campaign
Run 3-5 ad variations per audience
Kill: CTR below 0.5% after 1,000 impressions
Kill: CPA above 2x target after $50 spend
Kill: zero conversions after 5x expected CPA spend
Scale: CPA below target for 3+ consecutive days with consistent volume

section.title: Ad Copy Formulas

list.item: PAS: "Tired of [problem]? [Amplify pain]. [Product] solves this by [benefit]."
Before/After: "Before [Product]: [pain state]. After: [desired state]."
Social Proof: "Join [N]+ [audience] who [benefit]."

rule.statement: Use real product screenshots over stock photos. Ads with faces outperform faceless creative on Meta by 15-30% on average CTR.
rule.rationale: Authenticity signals reduce ad skepticism. Stock imagery is pattern-matched as advertising and ignored faster.

section.name: tracking-and-optimization
section.title: UTM Structure, Conversion Events, and Weekly Optimization Routine
section.p: UTM parameter naming convention, the 5 essential conversion events to track, and the weekly optimization cadence for managing active campaigns.

rule.statement: Use consistent UTM parameters on every paid link. Inconsistent UTM naming makes attribution analysis unreliable across campaigns.
rule.rationale: When utm_source values vary (meta vs Meta vs facebook), GA4 aggregates them as separate sources and understates channel performance.

example.note: ?utm_source=meta&utm_medium=paid&utm_campaign=summer-sale-2026&utm_content=video-v1. Use lowercase, hyphens not underscores, consistent source names per channel.

section.title: Essential Conversion Events

list.item: 1. Page View — baseline traffic measurement
2. Lead — signup or form submission
3. Trial Start — product trial initiated
4. Purchase — transaction completed
5. Subscription — recurring revenue started

rule.statement: Implement server-side Conversion API in addition to pixel tracking. Pixel data degrades with ad blockers and iOS privacy changes; server-side fills the gap.
rule.rationale: Without server-side tracking, Meta and Google can miss 20-40% of conversions, causing the algorithm to optimize on incomplete signals.

section.title: Weekly Optimization Routine

list.item: Monday: review last week's CPA, ROAS, CTR by campaign and ad set
Tuesday: pause underperformers, increase budget on winners by 20-30%
Wednesday: create new ad variations based on winning angles
Thursday: test new audience segments
Friday: analyze and document learnings; plan next week

antipattern.description: Increasing campaign budget by more than 30% in a single adjustment.
antipattern.reason: Large budget increases (50%+) trigger the platform learning phase, resetting algorithm optimization and causing temporary CPA spikes.

section.title: Key Metrics

list.item: CTR (Clicks / Impressions): target 1%+ for healthy ad relevance
CPC (Cost / Clicks): varies by platform and industry
CVR (Conversions / Clicks): target 2-5%
CPA (Cost / Conversions): must be below LTV/3
ROAS (Revenue / Ad Spend): target 3:1 minimum

template.purpose: Campaign planning template: goals, audience, creative, tracking, success criteria
Weekly ads report: spend, conversions, CPA, ROAS, learnings, next actions

section.name: limitations-and-gotchas
section.title: Where This Fails and AI-Agent Gotchas
section.p: Understanding the limitations of this framework is critical for avoiding costly mistakes.
section.title: Methodology Limitations

list.item: The methodology assumes platform conversion data is correct; iOS ATT, ad-blockers, and walled-garden self-reporting silently distort it.
LTV:CAC of 3:1 is a heuristic, not a law — subscription churn, refunds, fraud can flip a "winner" in month 3.
"Kill after 1000 impressions" works for high-intent search, fails for upper-funnel video where 10k impressions is the minimum signal.
Weekly cadence is too slow for fast-moving e-com promotions (Black Friday) and too fast for B2B with 90-day sales cycles.
Cross-channel attribution is hand-waved here — pair with ads-attribution-models for a defensible rollup.
Budget benchmarks ($500/mo Meta minimum) are channel-defaults; specific verticals (legal, finance) need 5-10x that.

section.title: AI-Agent Gotchas

list.item: LLMs love to "optimize" winning ads by tweaking copy mid-flight, killing performance. Lock winners for ≥7 days.
"Conversions" reported by platforms double-count; agents that sum across platforms report inflated revenue. Use GA4 / warehouse total.
Models often skip the LTV gate if the user gives them a budget directly. Hard-code the gate as a precondition.
Human-in-loop checkpoints: enabling a campaign, increasing daily budget by >25%, expanding to a new channel, opening a new geo.
Time-based reasoning is weak: agents will compare a 3-day-old campaign to a 90-day baseline. Force "min-data" assertions before judging.
Creative tone drift: agent-written ad copy on its 5th iteration becomes generic. Reset the creative agent with fresh brand-voice context.
Currency / FX: multi-region accounts mix USD/EUR/GBP cost rows; agents must normalize before computing CPA.
